definition: To actively support and argue for something — a design decision, a user need, or a process.
example: I advocate for accessibility early in the design process, before implementation starts.

definition: Unclear or incomplete specs that require you to ask questions and make informed assumptions.
example: When requirements are ambiguous, I try to clarify before writing a single line of code.

definition: Your method or strategy for solving a problem — interviewers love hearing about your approach.
example: My approach to design systems is to start with tokens before touching any components.

definition: To express your ideas clearly and precisely, especially technical or design decisions.
example: I try to articulate my design decisions so engineers understand the intent behind each choice.

definition: Able to work independently and make decisions without constant supervision.
example: I'm comfortable working autonomously — I manage my own priorities and deliverables.

definition: To weigh competing priorities — speed vs quality, design vs engineering constraints.
example: I constantly balance visual polish with implementation feasibility and timeline.

definition: To connect two different worlds — typically design and engineering — reducing friction between them.
example: As a UX Engineer, I bridge the gap between design intent and technical implementation.

definition: To create something starting from nothing rather than extending an existing solution.
example: I built the design system from scratch, including the token architecture and documentation.

definition: A difficulty or obstacle you encountered — often the setup for a strong interview story.
example: The biggest challenge was balancing performance with a visually rich interface.

definition: To work jointly with others — designers, engineers, PMs — toward a shared outcome.
example: I collaborate closely with designers to catch implementation issues early in the process.

definition: Explaining the pros and cons of a decision clearly so stakeholders can choose knowingly.
example: I always communicate trade-offs so the team understands what we gain and what we give up.

definition: A development approach that builds UIs as a composition of isolated, reusable components.
example: I work in a component-driven way — every piece of UI is designed to be reusable from the start.

definition: The quality of applying the same patterns, styles, and decisions throughout a product.
example: Consistency across the product is one of the things I care most about as a UX Engineer.

definition: To add value to a project, team, or codebase — a key word for describing past work.
example: I contributed to the component library by standardizing the button variants and documentation.

definition: Finding non-obvious solutions by combining technical and design thinking.
example: I enjoy creative problem-solving — especially when constraints push me toward unexpected solutions.

definition: Working across multiple disciplines simultaneously — design, code, and UX at once.
example: My background is cross-disciplinary — I think in systems but I also write production code.

definition: The process of choosing between options — often what interviewers probe with behavioral questions.
example: My decision-making process usually starts by understanding the constraints and user impact.

definition: A thorough, detailed investigation into a specific problem or topic.
example: I did a deep dive into our CSS architecture before proposing a migration to design tokens.

definition: Clarifying what the actual issue is before jumping to solutions — a key engineering and design skill.
example: Before jumping into code, I always take time to define the problem clearly.

definition: To complete and ship a working result — designs, features, or documentation.
example: I delivered the redesign on time while also improving the accessibility score.

definition: An instinctive awareness of what looks, feels, and functions well in a UI.
example: What makes me different as a frontend dev is that I bring a strong design sensibility to the code.

definition: Attentive to small specifics — spacing, edge cases, naming — that affect overall quality.
example: I'm very detail-oriented — I notice when something is 2px off and I care enough to fix it.

definition: To write clear records of decisions, processes, or code so others can understand and build on them.
example: I document my components thoroughly so other developers can use them without asking me questions.

definition: To lead or push something forward proactively — projects, decisions, or improvements.
example: I drove the migration from hardcoded styles to a token-based design system.

definition: A rehearsal or test run before the real thing — useful for presentations or demos.
example: I always do a dry run of my portfolio walkthrough before the actual interview.

definition: Covering the full scope of a process — from initial design concept to shipped production code.
example: I can own a feature end-to-end — from wireframes to deployed, accessible components.

definition: Approaching problems systematically, thinking about scalability, edge cases, and constraints.
example: I bring an engineering mindset to design — I ask 'what breaks this?' before shipping anything.

definition: To promote and build enthusiasm for a practice, tool, or approach within a team.
example: I evangelize accessibility practices within teams that don't yet prioritize it.

definition: To try new tools, approaches, or ideas in a controlled way to learn and validate.
example: I actively experiment with new technologies through personal projects before bringing them to work.

definition: Someone who acquires new skills and knowledge quickly, especially in new environments.
example: I'm a fast learner — I picked up Supabase and PostgreSQL through a real production project.

definition: Using input from others — users, teammates, reviewers — to guide and improve your work.
example: My design process is feedback-driven — I share work early and often to avoid big surprises.

definition: To solve problems independently by researching, experimenting, and reasoning through them.
example: I'm comfortable with ambiguity — I know how to figure things out when there's no clear answer.

definition: Highly proficient in a tool, language, or methodology — stronger than 'familiar with'.
example: I'm fluent in React and TypeScript — they're my primary tools for building interfaces.

definition: Your default, preferred tool or approach for a given type of problem.
example: My go-to stack for frontend projects is Next.js, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS.

definition: The belief that skills and intelligence can be developed through effort and learning.
example: I have a growth mindset — I'm always looking for gaps in my knowledge to work on next.

definition: Directly involved in doing the work rather than just directing or planning it.
example: I'm a hands-on designer — I prototype in code, not just in Figma.

definition: To take on different roles simultaneously — common in startups and small teams.
example: I'm used to wearing multiple hats — I can design, build, and write documentation for the same feature.

definition: To draw attention to the most important or impressive parts of your experience.
example: I'd like to highlight a project where I owned the full design system from tokens to components.

definition: Considering the full picture — user needs, technical constraints, and business goals together.
example: I take a holistic view of the product — not just the UI, but the whole user experience.

definition: Prioritizing work based on the value it delivers to users or the business.
example: I try to be impact-focused — I ask 'does this actually improve the experience?' before building.

definition: Code or features that are live and being used by real users — not just prototypes.
example: Everything in my portfolio is in production — I build things that actually get shipped.

definition: To act proactively without being asked — identifying problems and proposing solutions.
example: I took initiative to audit our component library and document the inconsistencies I found.

definition: A phrase to express genuine motivation for a role, team, or problem space.
example: I'm particularly interested in roles where design and engineering overlap.

definition: A natural, trained ability to notice specific qualities — detail, proportion, inconsistency.
example: I have a keen eye for visual inconsistencies that most engineers miss.

definition: To guide a project, decision, or team — even informally without a formal title.
example: I led the frontend architecture decisions for the redesign without having a lead title.

definition: To embrace and commit to something rather than avoid it — a strength, a challenge, a direction.
example: I lean into the ambiguity of being a UX Engineer — that's exactly where I add the most value.

definition: Code or systems that are easy to update, extend, and understand by other developers.
example: I write maintainable code — clear naming, minimal abstraction, and always documented.

definition: To guide and support someone less experienced in developing their skills.
example: I've informally mentored junior designers on how to think about component structure.

definition: Organizing work around concrete, measurable checkpoints rather than open-ended efforts.
example: I prefer milestone-driven projects — clear goals help me prioritize and ship consistently.

definition: What drives your energy and engagement — a key question in culture-fit interviews.
example: I'm motivated by problems that sit at the intersection of design quality and technical craft.

definition: To execute the small specifics with high precision — spacing, states, edge cases.
example: I care about nailing the details — hover states, loading states, error states — not just the happy path.

definition: Taking full responsibility for a task or area — from start to finish, including problems.
example: I take full ownership of my work — if something breaks in production, I'm the one who fixes it.

definition: A phrase expressing genuine enthusiasm — use sparingly and always back it with specifics.
example: I'm passionate about the space where design systems meet developer experience.

definition: Focused on practical solutions that work rather than theoretically perfect ones.
example: I'm pragmatic — I'd rather ship a good solution now than wait for a perfect one that never comes.

definition: Anticipating problems and acting before being asked rather than reacting after the fact.
example: I'm proactive about spotting UX issues before they reach the engineering queue.

definition: Documented history of successfully delivering results — backed by portfolio and examples.
example: I have a proven track record of shipping design systems that teams actually adopt.

definition: Delivering quality work in a short amount of time without sacrificing standards.
example: I can do a quick turnaround on prototypes — usually I can validate an idea same day.

definition: Improving something incrementally through iteration rather than getting it perfect at once.
example: My design system is something I've refined over time based on real usage and feedback.

definition: Equipped with the habits, tools, and communication skills to work effectively in a remote team.
example: I'm fully remote-ready — I'm async-first, I document everything, and I over-communicate by default.

definition: The measurable or observable outcomes of your work — what actually changed because of you.
example: I can speak to the results — after the redesign, task completion improved noticeably.

definition: Able to identify what needs to be done and pursue it without waiting for instructions.
example: I'm very self-directed — I built English Journal entirely on my own initiative.

definition: Quick to understand new concepts, catch issues, and find clever solutions — a compliment in tech.
example: Working in both design and code keeps me sharp — I never stop learning new things.

definition: To release and deploy a feature or product to real users — the ultimate goal of any dev work.
example: I've shipped everything in my portfolio — these aren't demos, they're real products.

definition: A personal project built outside of work — often the best signal of genuine passion in tech.
example: My side project English Journal lets me experiment with backend and AI features in a real context.

definition: Strong fundamental knowledge in a skill that allows you to build more advanced work on top.
example: I have a solid foundation in CSS — specificity, cascade, custom properties — not just utility classes.

definition: To address or provide evidence for something — used when backing up a claim with an example.
example: I can speak to my experience with design tokens — here's how I structured them in production.

definition: The skills and qualities where you consistently perform above average — know yours cold.
example: My strengths are at the intersection of visual design and component architecture.

definition: Someone who breaks down complex problems into clear, logical steps before acting.
example: I'm a structured thinker — I usually map out the problem space before writing any code.

definition: To care deeply about the quality of your work — not just getting it done but getting it right.
example: I take pride in writing CSS that reads like documentation — clear, predictable, and intentional.

definition: A thorough understanding of how something works beneath the surface, not just how to use it.
example: I have technical depth in CSS — I understand the cascade, not just how to override it.

definition: To perform exceptionally well in a particular environment or type of challenge.
example: I thrive in environments where design and engineering collaborate closely from day one.

definition: To convert design intent into working code, or technical constraints into design language.
example: My job is to translate Figma designs into accessible, performant React components.

definition: What specifically makes you different from other candidates applying for the same role.
example: My unique value is that I can review a pull request and a design spec in the same afternoon.

definition: Making decisions based on actual user needs and behaviors rather than assumptions.
example: I keep a user-centered perspective even when working deep in the codebase.

definition: Capable of adapting across different roles, tools, and types of problems effectively.
example: I'm versatile — I've worked on branding, design systems, frontend architecture, and AI integrations.

definition: A clear picture of where you want to take something — a product, a system, or your career.
example: My vision for the design system was a single source of truth that both designers and devs trust.

definition: An interviewer's request to explain a project or process step by step in your own words.
example: Sure, let me walk you through my approach to building the component library.

definition: Something actively being developed — honest framing when showing unfinished portfolio work.
example: This feature is a work in progress, but I can show you the current state and explain the direction.

definition: Shorthand for tenure — but quality and depth of experience matter more than raw years.
example: I have four years of experience, but the projects span far more in scope and complexity.
